title: State Management (Basic)

questions.question_text: Why shouldn't you modify state variables directly in React?
options: It won't trigger a re-render of the component
It is deprecated in newer versions
questions.explanation: React only knows to update the UI when the setter function from useState is called.

options: A global variable for the whole website
An object that holds information that may change over the lifetime of a specific component
questions.explanation: When state changes, the component automatically re-renders to reflect the new data.

questions.question_text: Which Hook is used to manage state in a functional component?
questions.explanation: useState returns a state variable and a function to update it.

questions.question_text: What happens when you call the 'setState' function?
questions.explanation: React schedules a re-render of the component and its children.

questions.question_text: How do you initialize a state variable 'count' to 0?
questions.explanation: This follows the destructuring pattern required by useState.

questions.question_text: Can you change state directly (e.g., count = count + 1)?
options: No, you must always use the setter function to trigger a re-render
questions.explanation: Direct mutation won't trigger React's rendering engine.

options: Creating a new copy of an object/array instead of modifying the existing one
questions.explanation: React relies on new object references to detect changes efficiently.

questions.question_text: How do you update an object in state `{ name: 'John', age: 25 }` to change only the age?
questions.explanation: The spread operator (...) copies the existing properties, then overrides the specific one.

questions.question_text: What is the result of `const [val, setVal] = useState();` (no initial value)?
questions.explanation: If no initial value is passed, the state is 'undefined'.

options: Moving all state to the very top component
Keeping state as close as possible to where it is being used
questions.explanation: Co-location improves performance and makes components easier to move/refactor.

options: Props for data that changes; State for data passed in
Props for data passed from parent; State for internal data that changes within the component
State is for the backend; Props is for CSS
questions.explanation: Props are like function arguments; State is like internal variables.

questions.question_text: What is a 'controlled component' in React forms?
options: A component that is password protected
An input whose value is determined by the React state
A component that has no styles
A component that cannot be moved
questions.explanation: React becomes the 'single source of truth' for the form value.

questions.question_text: True or False: useState updates are always synchronous.
questions.explanation: React may batch state updates for performance, so they behave asynchronously.

questions.question_text: How do you update state based on a previous value (e.g., incrementing)?
questions.explanation: The functional update pattern (prev => ...) is safer when updates rely on the current state value.

questions.question_text: What is the purpose of 'Batching' in React?
options: To make the code look messy
To group multiple state updates into a single re-render for better performance
To delete multiple states at once
To send data to the server in chunks
questions.explanation: React 18 introduced 'Automatic Batching' for almost all updates.

questions.question_text: Why shouldn't you use props directly in useState (e.g., `useState(props.user)`) unless it's for 'initial' only?
options: The state won't update automatically if the prop changes later; it only sets the value once at mount
questions.explanation: If you want it to stay synced, use the prop directly or 'useEffect'.
